coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent silver oxide/C- dots nanocomposite as selective and sensitive probe for highly efficient detection of Fe(III) ions
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Shelja
Mehta, S.K.
Kansal, Sushil Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Well crystalline Ag2O/C- dots quasispherical nanoparticles were synthesized by facile hydrothermal process assisted photo reaction. These were characterized in detail in terms of their morphological, structural, compositional and optical properties by UV–vis spectroscopy, Fourier transformation infra-red spectroscopy (FTIR), Photoluminescence (PL), Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Field Emission Scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Dynamic light scattering (DLS). These hydrophilic nanoparticles exhibit excellent photostability even after a month. The obtained Ag2O/C- dots exhibited a prominent emission at the wavelength of 455nm, at optimum excitation wavelength of 370nm. The quenching of fluorescence intensity Ag2O/C- dots upon addition of Fe3+ ions prompted to develop susceptible method for quantifying the presence of Fe3+ ions in aqueous medium. With optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity versus concentration of Fe3+ ions gave a linear correlation, as per Stern–Volmer equation. The linear range from the calibration curve and the limit of detection were found to be 0.09–1.66μM and 2.5nM, respectively. It was noteworthy to mention that the synthesized Ag2O/C- dots exhibit promising capability to be used as fluorescent probes for determination of Fe3+ ions in aqueous solutions, which is not interfered by the presence of other metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Silver
Fluorescence
Quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of Ag2O/C- dots
Influence of pH on fluorescence intensity of Ag2O/C- dots
Effect of NaCl concentration on the stability of Ag2O/C- dots
Influence of metal ions on fluorescence intensity of Ag2O/C- dots
Effect of Fe (III) ions on the fluorescence emission spectra of the Ag2O/C- dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A), (B) TEM images of bare c- dots at different magnification (C) and (D) TEM and FESEM image of Ag2O/C- dots.
XRD pattern of as synthesized nanocomposite.
(A) FTIR spectrum of nanocomposite (B) UV–vis spectrum of Ag2O/C- dots composite.
XPS measurements of Ag2O/C- dots (A) Full XPS survey (B) High resolution Ag 3d spectrum (C) High resolution O 1s spectrum (D) High resolution C 1s spectrum.
(A) Emission spectra of Ag2O/C- dots at various excitation wavelengths. (B) Photostability of Ag2O/C- dots as compared to fluorescence spectrum after a month.
(A) Variation in fluorescence intensity of Ag2O/C- dots with different pH. (B) Effect of different concentrations of NaCl on the fluorescence intensity of Ag2O/C- dots.
(A) Effect of different type of metal ions on fluorescence intensity of Ag2O/C- dots in distilled water. (B) Fluorescence quenching of Ag2O/C- dots upon addition of Fe (III) ions.
(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of Ag2O/C- dots with varied concentrations of Fe3+ ions. (B) Stern − Volmer plot for fluorescence quenching of Ag2O/C- dots by Fe (III) ions.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent silver oxide/C- dots nanocomposite as selective and sensitive probe for highly efficient detection of Fe(III) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Centre of Advanced Studies

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dr. S S Bhatnagar University Institute of Chemical Engg. & Technology, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014 India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr. S S Bhatnagar University Institute of Chemical Engg. & Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Well crystalline Ag2O/C- dots quasispherical nanoparticles were synthesized by facile hydrothermal process assisted photo reaction. These were characterized in detail in terms of their morphological, structural, compositional and optical properties by UV–vis spectroscopy, Fourier transformation infra-red spectroscopy (FTIR), Photoluminescence (PL), Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Field Emission Scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Dynamic light scattering (DLS). These hydrophilic nanoparticles exhibit excellent photostability even after a month. The obtained Ag2O/C- dots exhibited a prominent emission at the wavelength of 455nm, at optimum excitation wavelength of 370nm. The quenching of fluorescence intensity Ag2O/C- dots upon addition of Fe3+ ions prompted to develop susceptible method for quantifying the presence of Fe3+ ions in aqueous medium. With optimized conditions, the fluorescence intensity versus concentration of Fe3+ ions gave a linear correlation, as per Stern–Volmer equation. The linear range from the calibration curve and the limit of detection were found to be 0.09–1.66μM and 2.5nM, respectively. It was noteworthy to mention that the synthesized Ag2O/C- dots exhibit promising capability to be used as fluorescent probes for determination of Fe3+ ions in aqueous solutions, which is not interfered by the presence of other metal ions.

ce:section.ce:para: The development and design of fluorescent sensors for the detection of metal ions have gained utmost significance due to the vital and major roles played by metal ions in biology, environment, medicine and catalysis [1–3]. Among various metal ions, particular attention has been devoted to Fe (III) ions. Iron is the abounding and vital transition metal ion for human beings. Iron is utilized in number of proteins for electron and oxygen carrier and also as a catalyst in oxido- reductase reactions [4,5]. The inadequacy of iron leads to decrease in delivery of oxygen to cells, which subsequently results tiredness, affects work activity and immune system, also causes diseases like anemia, Alzheimer and Parkinson’s disease [6,7]. Moreover, if present in excessive amounts in a living cell, it can damage lipids, proteins and nucleic acids [8]. Therefore it becomes important to make efforts to develop fluorescent probes for the determination of Fe3+ ions because of their acute simplicity, high sensitivity and selectivity as fluorescent quenching is one of the most employed and successful method for detection and quantification of metal ions [9–11].

In past years, heavy metal based semiconductor quantum dots have been widely explored as fluorescent probes for optical sensing applications [12,13]. These have tendency to possess several amazing properties such as wide excitation spectrum, narrow emission spectrum, brightness and photochemical stability [14]. But from the safety point of view, its high toxicity in terms of environmental concerns has restricted its practical applications significantly [15]. As a new member of carbon family, carbon dots (C- dots) prove to be an alternative to conventional semiconductor quantum dots, and have attracted immense research interests in the field of material science after its erratic discovery [16]. C- dots are promising scaffolds with fantastic sizes less than 10nm having abundant oxygenous and hydrogenous residues [17]. The appearances of fluorescent C- dots have exhibited enormous impact on the advancement of various fields including energy, photonics, electronics, catalysis [18], biosensing [19], imaging [20], medicine and theranostics [21] and attracted growing interests attributable to their less-toxicity [22], excellent biocompatibility [23], tunable fluorescence and photostability, non-blinking behavior [24], high aqueous solubility [25], easy surface functionalization [26], resistance to environmental change [27], robust chemical inertness [28], and ease of preparation [29] which makes them different from semiconductor quantum dots such as CdTe, CdS etc. Likely the photoluminescent emission of semiconductor quantum dots, C- dots might also be corresponded to radiative recombination of photogenerated holes and electrons and the resulting photoexcited C- dots could serve the purpose as both electron donors and acceptors [30]. Carbon nanostructures have been largely inspected as supporting materials as these are endowed with extraordinary mechanical strength, high surface areas, favorable electrical conductivity, and durability even in severe conditions [31,32]. C- dots are compatible with solvents and thus have privileged in the assemblage of composite materials and optoelectronic appliances. There arises feasibility of growth of metal or metal oxide nanoparticles on the surface of C- dots. However, the fluorescence intensity of C- dots can be greatly enhanced by with metal/metal oxide nanoparticles also known as metal enhance fluorescence [33]. It is reported that the interaction of fluorophores with metal leads to enhanced photostability and photoluminescence intensities [34,35]. Zhang et al. reported the metal enhanced fluorescence of C- dots adsorbed Ag@SiO2 core shell nanoparticles and ascribed to surface plasmon resonance [36]. Amjadi et al. reported a fluorescent “turn- on” method for analysis of cysteine using C- dots/Ag nanoparticles [37]. Shen et al. reported a single step approach for the growth of silver nanoparticles on the surface of C- dots [38].

In the present work, we report a convenient hydrothermal assisted photoreaction synthesis of silver oxide/C- dots (Ag2O/C- dots) nanocomposite, which have been further, characterized and investigated for fluorescent sensing of Fe3+ ions. We hereby propose that this work will be beneficial to scientific community as practical application for highly sensitive and selective detective of Fe3+ ions employing Ag2O/C- dots as fluorescent probes.

All chemicals used in our experiments were of analytical grade and brought into use as received without any purification. Ethanol, acetone, sodium chloride and silver nitrate of analytical grades were purchased from Merck, India. Dichloromethane was procured from S. D. fine chem. ltd. (India). Inorganic salts such as ferric chloride, magnesium acetate, sodium chloride, strontium nitrate, lead nitrate were procured from Merck, India, and nickel nitrate, cadmium sulfide, zinc sulfate and aluminium sulfate were obtained from S. D. fine chem. ltd. (India). Deionized water (distilled) was used throughout the experiment.

The synthesized sample was characterized by various analytical and spectroscopic techniques. The general morphology of the synthesized nanoparticles was analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), using a TEM; JEOL-JEM-2100F and Field emission transmission electron microscopy (FESEM), using HITACHI, SU8010. The structure and crystallinity of the nanoparticles were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. These patterns were recorded on PANalytical Model X’Pert PRO with Cu-Kα radiation (k =1.54056Å) at voltage 45kV and current 40mA in the range of 2θ=100–800. The chemical composition and functional groups of the nanoparticles were determined by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy via Thermoscientific Nicolet iS50 equipped with a diamond ATR with KBr as a reference in the range of 500–4000cm−1. The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded in the range of 200–800nm using an UV–vis spectrophotometer (Systronics-2202). The optical characteristics of the synthesized nanoparticles were analyzed by room-temperature photoluminescence (PL) on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (5J1-004 model). The X- ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results were collected by AMICUS, Kratos Analytical (A Shimadzu Group Company) with monochromated Mg Kα (1253.6eV) source.

C- dots were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of freshly prepared pine apple juice without any preservative. In a brief methodology, 100mL pine apple juice (sieved) was mixed with 75mL ethanol and kept in a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave in an oven hydrothermally for three hours at 150°C. The as obtained product was then extracted with dichloromethane and then centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 20min with the addition of excess acetone. The heavier precipitates were discarded and the suspension of C- dots was obtained and preserved for further use.

In a typical synthesis, silver nitrate was added into appropriate amounts to the solution of C- dots (the molar ratio of silver nitrate: carbon dots 0.5% to 1%). The solution was kept under sonication for about half an hour. Then it was transferred to a quartz cuvette and kept under UV light (λ=254nm) for 120min. The change in the color of the solution from yellow to dark brown, indicating the formation of Ag2O/C- dots nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of Ag2O/C- dots
ce:section.ce:para: Stock solutions of different kinds of metal cations such as Pb2+, Na+, Cd2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Mg2+, Sr2+ and Zn2+ have been prepared in distilled water. All the dilute samples were prepared from standard stock aqueous solution of concentration of 0.1M of their respective metal ions. Briefly, an aqueous suspension of metal salt was mixed with 2mL of Ag2O/C- dots solution (concentration=10μg/mL) and incubated for a minute. The fluorescence spectra of the resultant solution were carried out at excitation wavelength (λex)=370nm and excitation and emission slit 10nm at PMT 700V.

The nanocomposite was synthesized by hydrothermal assisted photoreaction method. It was characterized in terms of its crystalline, compositional, optical, morphological, thermal and photoluminescent properties, etc. in detail.

For examining the general morphology, the prepared sample was examined by the Transmission electron microscopy and Field emission scanning electron microscopy. The TEM images of the bare C- dots and Ag2O/C- dots are shown in Fig. 1 . As it is clear from the images A and B, the bare C- dots are amorphous in nature and the size of bare C- dots are in the range 1–2nm. The TEM and FESEM images C, D of Ag2O/C- dots clearly indicated the quasispherical morphology, uniformly distributed and well separated nanoparticles from each other which are bigger in size than the bare C- dots. It was also reported that carbon dots helped in stabilizing the silver oxide nanoparticles and prevented its agglomeration [38,39]. The typical sizes of nanoparticles are less than 10nm.

Fig. 2 represents the typical XRD pattern of as prepared Ag2O/C- dots which displayed various well defined diffraction reflections. The presence of diffraction reflections at 2θ=27.90, 32.20 and 46.50 are associated with hexagonal phase of Ag2O and are ascribed to (110), (111) and (200) planes. The recognized diffraction reflections are in good agreement with the reported JCPDS card no. 42–0874 for hexagonal phase of pure Ag2O [40]. In addition to these peaks, a weak shoulder peak at 200 was also observed which is ascribed to graphitic structure of C- dots [41]. The regular crystallite sizes of the prepared nanoparticles were measured by Debye- Scherrer formula as discussed below:
D=k λ/β cos θ
where D is the crystallite size, k is the constant, λ is the X-ray wavelength, β is the full width half maximum and θ is the diffraction angle. According to the above mentioned equation, the average crystallite size is found to be 16nm.

FTIR spectrum of as synthesized sample is shown in Fig. 3 A. The broad peak at around 3278 and weak peak at 3700cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching of OH and NH bond respectively, and peak at 2923cm−1, 1535 and 1710cm−1 are ascribed to stretching of C H bond, bending of NH bond and stretching of CO group, respectively [42]. The peak at 530cm−1 corresponds the stretching vibration of AgO bond, confirms the formation of Ag2O nanoparticles [43]. Due to π-π* transitions of CC group and n-π* transitions of CO group, the UV- vis spectrum (Fig. 3B) of the yielded product reveals a broad absorption peak located at 285nm [44], moreover, due to the incorporation of silver oxide into C- dots, a prominent peak in between the 400–500nm was also observed, which is characteristic peak for surface plasmon resonance of silver [45].
The particle size of nanocomposite was calculated by dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement. The DLS data showed that the average particle sizes of nanocomposite was found to be 2.8nm

To inspect the chemical state of the elements in the product, XPS analysis was carried out for Ag2O/C- dots nanocomposite. Fig. 4 A shows the XPS survey, Ag 3d, Ag 3p, C 1s, O 1s XPS analysis spectra. The well-defined peaks corresponding to Ag, C and O can be distinctly seen in the spectrum. In the high resolution spectrum of Ag- 3d (Fig. 4B), the peaks located at 369.7eV and 375.9eV can be assigned to 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 spin orbit states, respectively. The XPS spectrum of O 1s in Fig. 4C exhibit the peak with binding energy of 534eV can be ascribed to lattice oxygen species of metal oxide. In the high resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 4D), the peaks related to CO and CC group can be obtained in good agreement with literature [46–49].

Fig. 5 A displays the fluorescence spectra of Ag2O/C- dots under different excitation wavelengths. As increase in excitation wavelength, it was observed that Ag2O/C- dots emitted at longer wavelengths. The results suggested that Ag2O/C- dots displayed excitation dependent phenomenon [50]. Also, the emission intensity showed highest peak at 465nm at excitation wavelength of 370nm. Hence, 370nm was chosen as the optimum excitation for further experimental procedures. The quantum confinement effect of Ag2O/C- dots is considered to be the prime cause of such strong emission. Further the comparison of fluorescence intensity was also recorded after a month. There was negligible and insignificant change in fluorescence intensity after 30days, indicating the stability of Ag2O/C- dots in aqueous medium (Fig. 5B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pH on fluorescence intensity of Ag2O/C- dots
ce:section.ce:para: Ag2O/C- dots were dissolved in distilled water and the influence of pH is considered to be one of the important factors in evaluating the nature of intensity of fluorescent materials. 0.1M NaOH and 0.1M HCl was employed to modify the pH values of Ag2O/C- dots solution. 2mL of resultant carbon dots solution of different pH ranging from 1 to 9 were prepared. Then the fluorescence spectra of different solutions were carried out as shown in Fig. 6 A. It was observed that the Ag2O/C- dots showed highest intensity at neutral pH=7, while it remains unchanged at basic or acidic conditions. At pH less than neutral, the decrease in fluorescence intensity might be attributed to the induced intramolecular hydrogen bonding of carboxylic acid moieties on the surface of the Ag2O/C- dots. Moreover at higher pH, deprotonation of carboxylic groups build negative charge on the surface, leading to the formation of anionic double layers. The existence of such double layers would disturb in the initiation of fluorescence [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of NaCl concentration on the stability of Ag2O/C- dots
ce:section.ce:para: Also, the influence of ionic strength on fluorescence intensity of synthesized sample was studied using various concentrations of NaCl. It was observed that ionic strength has no significant effect on fluorescence intensity and in the other characteristics of peak; rather it remained constant from 0.1M to 0.5M (Fig. 6B) [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of metal ions on fluorescence intensity of Ag2O/C- dots
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the selectivity of the proposed method, the effect of different metal ions on emission intensity of Ag2O/C- dots was investigated (Fig. 7 A). A significant selective quenching was observed in case of Fe3+ ions as compared to other metal ions, suggesting it could potentially be applied for analysis of Fe3+ ions along with extreme sensitivity.

The plausible explanation for this quenching can be considered as that the high number of valence electrons in outermost shell of Fe binds strongly and interacts effectively with Ag2O/C- dots, which consequently cause obtained high sensitivity of Ag2O/C- dots towards Fe3+ ions and exhibited significant quenching. Fe (III) ions could form metal hydroxides or carboxylates by specifically coordinating with the abundant hydroxyl and carboxylic groups of Ag2O/C- dots [53]. The complex formation of Fe3+ ions with Ag2O/C- dots at its surface could disturb the key element of the initial energy transition, responsible for emitting fluorescence which is the sheer case of static quenching. The complex formation phenomenon of Fe3+ ions on the Ag2O/C- dots surfaces can induce electron transfer quenching while the oxidation of Fe ions from +3 to +2 states occurred. Moreover, the resulting coordinated complex formed, Fe- Ag2O/C- dots might also facilitate the charge transfer from Ag2O/C- dots to Fe (III) leading to significant fluorescence quenching behavior. Other potential interfering ions act as poor quenchers for Ag2O/C- dots in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of Fe (III) ions on the fluorescence emission spectra of the Ag2O/C- dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 A exhibits the effect on emission spectra of Ag2O/C- dots with addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ ions from up to down with respective volumes 0–400μL. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually upon introduction of specific amounts of Fe3+ ions.

Keeping in view the significant amount of quenching of fluorescence intensity, the practicability of designing sensitive techniques for detection of Fe (III) ions based on spectrofluorometry has been taken into account. It is clearly seen in Fig. 8A that in emission spectra, there is absence of any kind of shift located around 455nm, which signifies that the conclusive and plausible mechanism is ascribed to electron transfer. The experimental data were fitted to the Stern–Volmer relation in equation given below:
F0/F=1+Ksv [Q]
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities obtained in absence and presence of Fe3+ ions, respectively, [Q] is the analyte concentration i.e. Fe3+ ions and K sv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant of the quencher. The Stern- Volmer relationship fits a good linear correlation with in the concentration range of 0.099- 1.66μM with R2 =0.994, verified the static quenching mode due to the formation of non- fluorescent complex. The detection limit was calculated as three times the standard deviation of the corrected measurements of the calibration curve (at a signal to noise ratio of 3). The Stern Volmer quenching constant was found to be 1.6×105 M−1 with lowest detection limit 2.568nM which is pretty lower than the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency allowed maximum tolerance (5.4μM) in drinking water. This order of magnitude is compatible with the formation of quite stable complexes (static quenching) between Ag2O/C- dots and Fe (III) ions [54]. However, when the concentration of Fe3+ is higher, the plot tends to obey non-linear behavior (Supporting Information, Fig. S1), depicting some degree of dynamic quenching as well [55]. Hamishekar et al. reported efficient detection of Fe3+ ions with the aid of C- dots in aqueous media with detection limit 6μM spectrofluorometrically [56]. Xu et al. reported sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe (III) ions with detection limit 0.5nM [57]. Relevant and in matter to LOD, the results are found to be in complete competence to other findings, suggesting the designed method has excellent sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe (III) ions.

A convenient method for detection of Fe3+ ions has been devised, depending on the quenching of the fluorescence intensity of Ag2O/C- dots. The synthesis method is quite facile. Under all optimized parameters, Ag2O/C- dots can be used to analyze Fe (III) ions with extreme specificity and sensitivity in an aqueous medium. Its highly selective nature doesn’t affect by the presence of other metal ions, at excitation wavelength of 370nm in an aqueous medium. The high fluorescence intensity of Ag2O/C- dots is based upon the metal enhanced fluorescence phenomenon. A stable linear relationship was obtained between fluorescence intensity of the system and concentration of Fe3+ ions with much lower detection limit 2.56nM can be achieved. The practical applicability of proposed fluorescent probe show promising potentials in environmental determination.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors greatly acknowledge the financial support received under the UGC − BSR Government of India through a grant No. F. 25-1/2013(BSR)/5- 91/2007 (BSR) and TEQIP-II grant of Dr. S. S. Bhatnagar UICET, Panjab University, Chandigarh. The authors would also like to thank SAIF, Panjab University, Chandigarh for providing required instrumentation facility.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.12.100.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

Detection of Sn(II) ions via quenching of the fluorescence of carbon nanodots
Fabrication of rare- earth/quantum- dot nanocomposites for color tunable sensing and applications

Synthetic developments of nontoxic quantum dots

Functional surface engineering of C-dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging
Mg/N double doping strategy to fabricate extremely high luminescent carbon dots for bioimaging
Competitive performance of carbon quantum dots in optical bioimaging
Systematic safety evaluation on photoluminescent carbon dots

Commercially activated carbon as the source for producing multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots by chemical oxidation
A green synthesis of carbon nanoparticle from honey for real-time photoacoustic imaging
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots for chemical sensing
Carbon quantum dots: synthesis, properties and applications
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Electric field effect in atomically thin carbon films

Metal-enhanced fluorescence from silver nanoparticle deposited polycarbonate substrates

Growth and stabilization of silver nanoparticles on carbon dots and sensing application

Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: synthesis characterization, and bioimaging application
Plant leaf-derived fluorescent carbon dots for sensing, patterning and coding
Carbon nanodots: synthesis: properties and applications

Economical, green route to highly fluorescence intensity carbon materials based on ligninsulfonate/graphene quantum dots composites: application as excellent fluorescent sensing platform for detection of Fe3+ ions

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shelja Sharma received her MSc in Chemistry from Thapar University, Patiala in 2013. She is currently a Ph.D student in the Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh since 2013. Her main research interest is focused on the study of synthesis and photoluminescence of carbon dots, as well as their applications in the field of photocatalysis and fluorescent sensing.

Dr. Sushil Kumar Kansal is an academician and researcher of international recognition at the Dr. S. S. Bhatnagar University Institute of Chemical Engg. & Technology, Panjab University, Chandigarh, India. His research areas are environmental science and engineering, biomaterials, advanced oxidation processes, nanotechnology, water pollution and material science. He has published more than 65 research papers in international and national journals of repute. One of the research publication which was published in 2007 has more than 538 citations. Dr. Kansal has visited many countries viz. China, Germany, Canada, UK, and Singapore. Dr. Kansal has guided 3 Ph.D’s and 12 Master students. He has completed 1 MHRD, 1 DST; 1 AICTE; 1 UGC projects. He was mentor from India for one training school on Wastewater Treatment at University of Hull, UK sponsored by DST/British Council under Newton Bhabha Fund (2016).

Dr. Surinder Kumar Mehta, Senior Professor Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh and Director SAIF/CIL/UCIM is highly active in the significant areas of research like metallosurfactant chemistry, nano electrochemical sensors, synthesis and application of semiconducting nanoparticles and colloidal chemistry that includes novel formations for drug delivery applications. He is also Coordinator, Chandigarh Region Innovation Knowledge Cluster (CRIKC), Local coordinator MHRD initiative GIAN, Coordinator UGC CAS and Chief Editor Panjab University Research Journal (Science). Dr. Mehta has more than 236 publications in international journals of repute with h-index of 30, i10 99 and is an author of about 10 books/chapters. He has been nominated as member of several DST, CSIR and UGC national committees. He is receipt of renowned DAAD and JSPS fellowships several times, Bronze medal from Chemical Research Society of India (CRSI), authors award by Royal Society of Chemistry (UK), Haryana Vigyan Ratna award and Prof. W.U. Malik Memorial Award of Indian Council of Chemists (ICC) for the year 2015 for his outstanding contribution in research. He has been a visiting scientist to several countries including UK, Germany, Japan, USA and France. Dr. Mehta has guided 10 post-doctoral, 30 Ph.D. and 40 Master students. He has completed/completing 5 DST; 4 CSIR; 6 UGC, 2 Indo-German and one Indo-Japan Research/Exchange projects. He was lead Coordinator from India for one training school on Waste water Treatment at University of Hull, UK sponsored by DST/British Council under Newton Bhabha Fund (2016). Recently he has been awarded a Fellowship of the Royal Society of Chemistry (FRSC) by the Royal Society of Chemistry (RSC).
